q: importance the use of acid etch before restoration
options: B- decrease the staining of dentin
explanation: Acid etching creates micromechanical retention and improves the enamel–resin seal, which decreases microleakage at restoration margins.

q: MRSA virus infection by
explanation: MRSA transmits primarily by direct contact with colonized skin/wounds or contaminated hands and fomites, not classic airborne droplet nuclei like measles.

q: gram negative bacteria Associated with
explanation: Subgingival overhanging margins trap plaque and favor anaerobic gram-negative periodontal pathogens; smooth or supragingival contours are less plaque-retentive.

q: years pedo came to the clinc with thumb sucking and her parnts tray with negative reinforcement but didn't help , what is your? manegment
explanation: When parental behavior modification fails for persistent thumb-sucking, a fixed habit-breaking appliance such as a palatal crib is the standard dental intervention.

q: Bruxer pt with mouth guard
explanation: A full-coverage hard nightguard for bruxism should provide even occlusal contacts on both anterior and posterior teeth to distribute load and stabilize the appliance.

q: the other name for organic occlusion
explanation: ‘Organic occlusion’ in classic occlusion teaching is mutually protected occlusion: posterior teeth protect anteriors in MIP, anteriors disclude posteriors in excursions.

q: Lower premolar with class I amalgam came to the clinic with broken distal cusp. What is the cause
explanation: Fracture of a cusp adjacent to a Class I amalgam typically follows undermined enamel/dentin left without adequate support after cavity preparation, not solely high occlusion.

q: you do caries risk assessment before perio treatment why
explanation: Caries risk assessment before periodontal therapy anticipates root exposure after scaling/surgery; exposed roots in high-risk patients drive sensitivity and root caries planning.

q: in lower premolar with pain while releasing from occlusion
explanation: Pain on release of occlusal pressure (rebound) is classic for a cracked tooth; vertical root fracture usually presents with deep isolated pockets and J-shaped bone loss after endo.

q: Patient to do implant of 11 , what is essential
explanation: Planning a single anterior implant (#11) requires prosthetically driven workup: diagnostic casts/wax-up and a surgical template/guide—more essential than closed-tray technique alone.

q: What is the cause of gross (ismuth )amalgam fracture
explanation: Bulk or isthmus fracture of amalgam is most often from inadequate preparation (insufficient depth/width, sharp internal angles, or weak isthmus), not diet investigation or veneer choices.

q: pt 5 years old with negative behavior and multiple occlusal caries
explanation: In a young uncooperative child, fluoride varnish painted/wiped on is preferred over tray gels (less gag, less ingestion risk, better behavior tolerance) for professional topical fluoride.

q: best impression material for final crown
explanation: Addition silicone (PVS) is preferred for final crown impressions: high accuracy, dimensional stability, and detail. Alginate lacks precision; agar is less practical for routine fixed work.

q: obturation material for patient allergic to resin
explanation: Among listed obturation-related options, resilon-type systems are the only root-filling choice present; clinically, true resin allergy favors gutta-percha with a non-resin sealer when available.

q: during RPD framework everything was perfect except the rest was smaller than the rest seats intra orally, you return it to the cast and it was perfect on the cast, what is your management A-soldering rest
options: C-redo the framework on the same cast
B- redo final impression and the framework
explanation: If the RPD rest fits the cast but not the mouth, the cast does not represent the patient (impression/cast error). Remake the final impression and framework; redoing on the same cast repeats the error.

q: pt has dark intrinsic staining, best type of veneers
explanation: Dark intrinsic stains need opaque, thin, durable bonded ceramic; etched porcelain veneers mask discoloration better than translucent composite and better than thick zirconia veneers in many board keys.

q: tooth #21 with substandard RCT with cast post and core well crowned ( it was written like this in the )exam
explanation: When a substandard RCT is sealed under a well-fitting cast post–core and crown, orthograde retreatment is difficult; apical surgery with root-end preparation and retrofill is the usual definitive path.

q: Why does large restoration complicate ortho tx
explanation: Large or poorly retained restorations complicate orthodontics mainly because brackets/bonds and restorations can fracture or fail during debonding and appliance removal, not by speeding tooth movement.

q: years old female Pt came to replace non restorable tooth with implant after radiographic examination there was multiple punched out radiolucency that are different in size what is your diagnosis
explanation: Multiple punched-out radiolucencies of varying size in an adult skull/jaws are classic for multiple myeloma (or metastatic disease); Gorlin is typically fewer odontogenic keratocysts in a younger patient.

q: Pt has amalgam restorations done 3 years ago, came with flat shiny surfaces and elevated amalgam restorations, whats your management
explanation: Proud, flat, shiny amalgams usually mean surrounding enamel/dentin was lost to acid erosion, so the restoration stands high; investigate GI reflux and dietary acids rather than replacing or raising VDO first.

q: Influenza virus infection by: Airborne- Droplets is a better answer
explanation: Among usable options, influenza transmission involves close respiratory exposure; droplet/contact precautions apply. (True primary mode is respiratory droplets/aerosols rather than blood-borne contact alone.)

q: Pt came to the clinic with all teeth missing except #17 , #33, #43 , #44 what’s : the best approach for Tx A-Extraction for #17 and Complete for U and Transitional for L B-Extraction for #17, #33,#43#44 and Complete UL C-Extraction for #17 , #14 and complete for U and L
explanation: With few remaining strategic teeth (#17, 33, 43, 44), preserving roots/abutments for an overdenture improves retention, proprioception, and ridge preservation versus extracting everything for conventional CDs.

q: type force of porcelain and metal in PFM
explanation: In PFM design, porcelain is brittle in tension but strong in compression; the metal substructure is shaped so porcelain is kept under compressive loading during function, reducing tensile fracture of the veneer.

q: you have amalgam and it has ditch with more 0.5 what is your management
options: B- place composite in the ditched area
explanation: Amalgam ditching deeper than about 0.5 mm indicates marginal breakdown and leakage risk; patching with composite over old amalgam is unreliable. Full replacement of the restoration is the appropriate management.

q: omposite restoration with undecontoure and open contact, margins are sealed and perfect, what is your management
options: A-repair it by removing part of the proximal box
explanation: When margins are sealed but the composite is undercontoured with open contact, conservative proximal repair (refresh/repair the box and rebuild contact) is preferred over full replacement if the remainder is sound.

q: Restoration not used as final restoration: CAVIT
options: Restoration not used as final restoration: CAVIT
explanation: Cavit is a temporary zinc oxide–based endodontic provisional only. It lacks strength, seal longevity, and wear resistance for definitive restoration and must be replaced with a proper temporary or permanent material.

q: fractured cusp , vital tooth
explanation: A fractured cusp on a vital tooth is ideally restored with cuspal coverage such as an onlay (ceramic/onlay coverage) to protect remaining structure; full crown is more aggressive, simple composite often underprotects.

q: Most common hypersensitivity with recession
explanation: Dentin hypersensitivity with gingival recession localizes to exposed cervical root dentin (open tubules near the CEJ), not mid-root bulk or crown enamel. Cervical root is the classic site of recession-related sensitivity.

q: Pt came with very deep caries you suspect that it is going to need crown lengthening what is the appropriate sequence
explanation: Sequence for deep caries possibly needing crown lengthening: excavate to assess restorability, take bitewings to evaluate remaining tooth structure/ferrule needs, complete RCT if indicated, then surgical crown lengthening.

q: chemo-mechanical caries removal
explanation: Carisolv is a chemomechanical caries-removal system (sodium hypochlorite-based gel with amino acids) used with hand instruments to soften and excavate carious dentin selectively without rotary cutting as the primary method.
